lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Come, cousin, canst thou quake, and change thy colour,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Murder thy breath in the middle of a word,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And then begin again, and stop again,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As if thou wert distraught and mad with terror?

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Tut, I can counterfeit the deep tragedian;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Speak and look back, and pry on every side,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Tremble and start at wagging of a straw,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Intending deep suspicion: ghastly looks

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Are at my service, like enforced smiles;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And both are ready in their offices,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: At any time, to grace my stratagems.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But what, is Catesby gone?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He is; and, see, he brings the mayor along.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Lord mayor—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Look to the drawbridge there!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Hark! a drum.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Catesby, o’erlook the walls.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Lord mayor, the reason we have sent—

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Look back, defend thee, here are enemies.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: God and our innocency defend and guard us!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Be patient, they are friends, Ratcliff and Lovel.

lines.speaker: LOVEL
lines.text: Here is the head of that ignoble traitor,

lines.speaker: LOVEL
lines.text: The dangerous and unsuspected Hastings.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So dear I loved the man, that I must weep.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I took him for the plainest harmless creature

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That breathed upon this earth a Christian;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Made him my book, wherein my soul recorded

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The history of all her secret thoughts:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So smooth he daub’d his vice with show of virtue,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That, his apparent open guilt omitted,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I mean, his conversation with Shore’s wife,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He lived from all attainder of suspect.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Well, well, he was the covert’st shelter’d traitor

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: That ever lived.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Would you imagine, or almost believe,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Were’t not that, by great preservation,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: We live to tell it you, the subtle traitor

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This day had plotted, in the council-house

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To murder me and my good Lord of Gloucester?

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: What, had he so?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What, think you we are Turks or infidels?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Or that we would, against the form of law,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Proceed thus rashly to the villain’s death,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But that the extreme peril of the case,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The peace of England and our persons’ safety,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Enforced us to this execution?

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: Now, fair befall you! he deserved his death;

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: And you my good lords, both have well proceeded,

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: To warn false traitors from the like attempts.

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: I never look’d for better at his hands,

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: After he once fell in with Mistress Shore.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Yet had not we determined he should die,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Until your lordship came to see his death;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which now the loving haste of these our friends,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Somewhat against our meaning, have prevented:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Because, my lord, we would have had you heard

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The traitor speak, and timorously confess

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The manner and the purpose of his treason;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That you might well have signified the same

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Unto the citizens, who haply may

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Misconstrue us in him and wail his death.

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: But, my good lord, your grace’s word shall serve,

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: As well as I had seen and heard him speak:

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: And doubt you not, right noble princes both,

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: But I’ll acquaint our duteous citizens

lines.speaker: MAYOR
lines.text: With all your just proceedings in this cause.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And to that end we wish’d your lordship here,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To avoid the carping censures of the world.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: But since you come too late of our intents,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Yet witness what you hear we did intend:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And so, my good lord mayor, we bid farewell.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Go, after, after, cousin Buckingham.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The mayor towards Guildhall hies him in all post:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: There, at your meet’st advantage of the time,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Infer the bastardy of Edward’s children:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Tell them how Edward put to death a citizen,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Only for saying he would make his son

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Heir to the crown; meaning indeed his house,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which, by the sign thereof, was termed so.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Moreover, urge his hateful luxury,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And bestial appetite in change of lust;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which stretched to their servants, daughters, wives,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Even where his lustful eye or savage heart,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Without control, listed to make his prey.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nay, for a need, thus far come near my person:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Tell them, when that my mother went with child

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of that unsatiate Edward, noble York

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My princely father then had wars in France;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And, by just computation of the time,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Found that the issue was not his begot;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which well appeared in his lineaments,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Being nothing like the noble duke my father:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But touch this sparingly, as ’twere far off;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Because you know, my lord, my mother lives.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Fear not, my lord, I’ll play the orator

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: As if the golden fee for which I plead

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Were for myself: and so, my lord, adieu.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If you thrive well, bring them to Baynard’s Castle;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Where you shall find me well accompanied

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With reverend fathers and well-learned bishops.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I go; and towards three or four o’clock

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Look for the news that the Guildhall affords.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Go, Lovel, with all speed to Doctor Shaw;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To Catesby. Go thou to Friar Penker; bid them both

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Meet me within this hour at Baynard’s Castle.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Now will I in, to take some privy order,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To draw the brats of Clarence out of sight;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And to give notice, that no manner of person

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: At any time have recourse unto the princes.

stage_directions.text: Enter Gloucester and Buckingham, in rotten armour, marvellous ill-favoured.
Enter Lovel and Ratcliff, with Hastings’ head.

glosses.definition: tremble and change colour — Richard's stage-direction to Buckingham's performance
imitate the accomplished tragic actor — Buckingham names the scene's mode as acting

glosses.phrase: Tremble and start at wagging of a straw
glosses.definition: the actor's technique: react to the least stimulus

glosses.phrase: history of all her secret thoughts
glosses.definition: Richard's bravura hypocrisy: he loved Hastings so that he recorded all his thoughts there
legal taint of suspicion — 'attainder' = the corruption of blood following a treason conviction
the two Elizabethan bywords for lawless unbelievers — Richard claims Christian legality
the official claim that Edward IV's sons are illegitimate — the legal pretext for Richard's accession
perform as public speaker — Buckingham's theatrical self-consciousness continues
Richard's other London residence, on the Thames — site of the 3.7 staged-reluctance scene
Ralph Shaw, cleric who preached the Edward-was-a-bastard sermon at Paul's Cross; historical figure
John Penker, provincial of the Augustinian friars; another propagandist cleric
Clarence's children — Margaret Pole and Edward, Earl of Warwick; Richard has them confined

annotations.title: The play explicitly names itself as theatre
annotations.body: Richard's opening line — "Come, cousin, canst thou quake, and change thy colour, / Murder thy breath in the middle of a word" — and Buckingham's reply ("I can counterfeit the deep tragedian") is one of the most bald metatheatrical exchanges in all of Shakespeare. The two leading villains are here directly discussing their own craft as actors. Buckingham's self-description — tremble and start at wagging of a straw, ghastly looks at my service, enforced smiles — is a textbook for the Elizabethan tragedian. Shakespeare is doing something striking: he is letting his villains announce that the whole political spectacle they are staging is theatre. The scene self-consciously rehearses for the Mayor's benefit what it in fact is for us: a play. The distance between Richard's public performance and Richard's private self is not concealed from the audience — it is dramatized.
sources: Anne Righter, Shakespeare and the Idea of the Play (1962)
Robert Weimann, Shakespeare and the Popular Tradition in the Theater (1967)

annotations.title: Doctor Shaw, Friar Penker, and the bastardy campaign
annotations.body: Richard's instructions to Buckingham — "Go, Lovel, with all speed to Doctor Shaw; / To Catesby. Go thou to Friar Penker" — name two historical propagandists. Ralph Shaw (the "Doctor" of divinity) preached at Paul's Cross on 22 June 1483 that Edward IV's children were illegitimate (arguing that Edward had been pre-contracted to Eleanor Butler before marrying Elizabeth Woodville). John Penker, provincial of the Augustinian friars, preached similar arguments. The sermon-campaign was the theological-legal groundwork for Richard's accession ten days later. Shakespeare compresses the campaign into a few stage-lines, but the detail is exact. What the play makes dramatically visible is the machinery: Buckingham delivers the propaganda at Guildhall (reported back in 3.7); clergymen stand ostentatiously next to Richard during the staged-reluctance tableau. Legitimacy is being manufactured in public view.
sources: Rosemary Horrox, Richard III: A Study of Service (1989)

trials.prompt: When Buckingham claims he can "counterfeit the deep tragedian," what is the scene doing dramaturgically?
options: Breaking down — the actors have lost their lines
Explicitly naming itself as theatre — the villains are discussing their own acting craft in front of the audience
Introducing a minor character (the tragedian) who will later speak
Citing a specific contemporary actor of the Lord Chamberlain's Men

trials.prompt: Who are Doctor Shaw and Friar Penker, sent for at this scene's end?
options: Fictional comic characters invented by Shakespeare
Historical clergymen who preached sermons arguing for Edward IV's children's illegitimacy — the theological groundwork for Richard's claim
Disguised officers of Richmond's intelligence service
Rival candidates for the see of Canterbury
